notes: Cap figures are extremely fluid due to the massive Tua Tagovailoa post-June 1 dead cap split ($67.4M in 2026, $31.8M in 2027). The Dolphins entered 2026 approximately $17M over the cap pre-cuts, then cleared over $56M via the Hill/Chubb/Daniels/Westbrook-Ikhine releases. The Willis signing ($22.25M signing bonus spread) and Tua dead money leave Miami with minimal effective cap room in 2026 before rookie contracts. This is a recognized reset/rebuild year under GM Jon-Eric Sullivan and HC Jeff Hafley.

key_departures.impact: Released with post-June 1 designation, creating a $67.4M dead cap hit in 2026 and $31.8M in 2027. Was the team's franchise QB but benched late in 2025 after a career-high 15 INTs. His departure was the catalyst for the entire rebuild. The Dolphins owe him $54M in guaranteed money for 2026, offset by any salary he earns elsewhere.

key_departures.new_team: TBD/unsigned (recovering from dislocated knee and torn ACL)
key_departures.impact: Released February 16, 2026. Was rehabbing a major leg injury and it was unclear if he'd play in 2026. Would have carried a $51.9M cap hit. His release clears massive cap space but leaves the WR room severely depleted. Had been the team's WR1 and one of the NFL's best receivers in 2022-2023. An elite weapon is now completely gone from the offense.

Released as a post-June 1 cut, saving $20M in 2026 cap space against $23.8M in dead money. Led the team in sacks in 2025 with 8.5. Combined with Jaelan Phillips being traded at the 2025 deadline, the EDGE room is now essentially empty beyond Chop Robinson, who has yet to prove himself as a true starter. A catastrophic departure for a position group that was a strength just six months ago.

key_departures.new_team: Carolina Panthers (4yr/$120M, $80M gtd — signed in FA after leaving PHI)
key_departures.impact: Was traded to the Eagles at the 2025 trade deadline for a 3rd-round pick. Phillips then signed a massive 4yr/$120M deal with Carolina in free agency. He was a key part of the pass rush before the deadline trade. The Dolphins received a 3rd-round pick in return, but losing him compounded the EDGE problem that now exists in 2026.

key_departures.new_team: New York Jets (trade: MIA receives 2026 7th-round pick; FitzPatrick signs 3yr/$40M extension with NYJ)
key_departures.impact: Traded to the Jets for a 2026 seventh-round pick — widely considered an underwhelming return. Was the team's best safety and started 14 games in 2025. Graded as PFF's 5th-best safety among 91 qualifiers. His departure creates a major void at safety. The Dolphins received only a 7th in return and the trade was to a division rival, drawing criticism.

Released February 2026 as part of the wave of cost-cutting moves. Had signed a 3yr/$24M deal with the Dolphins in 2025 but suffered a major injury in the 2025 season opener, playing very little. Was a legitimate starting guard when healthy. The Dolphins let him go after getting essentially nothing from the investment. Deepens the already-critical IOL need.

Departed in free agency, signing with the Chargers (where former Dolphins HC Mike McDaniel is now OC). Was on the Dolphins' roster as an IOL depth piece. His departure further thins an already stripped-down offensive line interior.
Released March 2, 2026 with a failed physical designation. Was an IOL depth piece.

Was an 11-game starter at right tackle in 2025 filling in for injured Austin Jackson. Acquitted himself well and would have been a reasonable depth/swing tackle option. His departure is partially offset by Austin Jackson remaining on the roster for 2026 at RT. Reduces depth but does not create a major new need given Jackson's return.
Released February 2026. Was a depth/rotational WR with limited impact. Combined with Tyreek Hill's release, the WR room is severely depleted.
Was Miami's punter for 3 seasons and had his best season in 2025 (47.7 yards/punt avg). His departure opens a special teams need. A replacement will need to be found via draft or veteran signing.

Released by the Dolphins, saved $3.06M against the cap. A team captain and Pro Bowl fullback in 2023, he joins ex-Dolphins HC Mike McDaniel in LA. The Dolphins shift away from using a fullback as part of the new scheme under Hafley/Slowik, so this is a scheme-driven departure rather than a need-creator.
Released March 2026. Was Miami's franchise kicker for 7 seasons (84.6% FG, best in franchise history). Sides were too far apart on a new contract. Creates a need at kicker.

Became a UFA when the new league year opened. Was limited to 9 games in 2025 due to hip and groin injuries. It was uncertain whether he would even play in 2026. He was a valuable red-zone weapon when healthy (6 TDs in 2025), but his departure was expected given his injury history and contract situation.

signings.contract_notes: 3yr/$67.5M with $45M fully guaranteed over the first two years. $22.25M signing bonus, $1.25M salary in 2026 (front-loaded bonuses make cap-friendly in Year 1), ramping to $21.5M salary in 2027. 2026 and 2027 salaries fully guaranteed. Per Tom Pelissero via The Phinsider.
signings.analysis: Miami struck on Day 1 of the legal tampering period, immediately replacing Tagovailoa with Willis, who followed HC Jeff Hafley from Green Bay. The 3yr/$45M guaranteed structure is a genuine commitment — this is not a bridge deal. However, Willis is unproven as a full-time NFL starter. In 11 games with Green Bay, he threw for 972 yards, 6 TDs, 0 INTs, but only started 3 games. His dual-threat ability fits the new offense under Bobby Slowik. The contract ($22.5M AAV) sits at the lower end of the starter tier, reflecting both the market value and the uncertainty about his ceiling. QB goes from a Tier 1 critical need to a Tier 2 need — they have their starter, but questions remain about long-term reliability and a backup is still needed. The team may still want to add a developmental QB via the draft given Sullivan's stated philosophy of drafting a QB 'every year if not every other year.'

signings.contract_notes: 1yr/$3.25M per ESPN's Jeremy Fowler. Guarantees not confirmed; treated as minimal/unspecified based on deal structure.
signings.analysis: Re-signed on a modest 1-year deal worth $3.25M, a raise from the just over $1M he earned in 2025. Dulcich was productive in 10 games with the Dolphins (26 catches, 335 yards, 1 TD). He is the de facto TE1 after Darren Waller's departure. However, a 1-year deal at well below the starter AAV tier ($3.25M vs. $8-14M starter range) signals this is a bridge arrangement. The Dolphins still need a longer-term TE solution — this minimally addresses the need and keeps it alive for the draft.

signings.contract_notes: Re-signed on a standard contract per the official team site (Miami Dolphins official announcement, March 4, 2026). Exact dollar terms not publicly disclosed; expected to be at or near the veteran minimum given his limited production.
signings.analysis: Goode has appeared in 37 games for Miami over three seasons but has been a fringe contributor, not a starter. Re-signing him is roster-management, not a solution. With Bradley Chubb and Jaelan Phillips both gone, and Chop Robinson as the only player with notable NFL EDGE experience remaining on the roster, the EDGE need is critical and Goode does nothing material to address it. EDGE remains the most urgent defensive need heading into the draft.

signings.contract_notes: Tendered as an exclusive rights free agent on March 2, 2026. Retains him at league minimum per ERFA rules.
signings.analysis: Exclusive rights tender keeps a developmental CB on the roster at minimum cost. Does not address the CB need in any meaningful way. The starting CB group remains entirely unsettled with virtually all experienced CBs (Douglas, Jack Jones, Artie Burns, Kohou) hitting free agency and no replacements signed.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Willis signing (3yr/$67.5M, $45M gtd) gives the Dolphins a legitimate starter with real guaranteed money invested. He is not a bridge deal — both 2026 and 2027 salaries are fully guaranteed. However, Willis is unproven as a full-time starter (3 starts, 11 games in NFL career) and the team's new GM has explicitly stated he wants to draft a QB 'every year if not every other year.' QB drops from Tier 1 (critical) to Tier 2 — they have their guy for now, but adding a developmental QB in the mid-rounds of the draft is still likely and appropriate. Do not remove QB from the needs board entirely.

The EDGE situation has actually worsened relative to pre-FA projections. Bradley Chubb (8.5 sacks in 2025, team leader) is released. Jaelan Phillips was traded at the 2025 deadline. Cameron Goode re-sign is cosmetic. Chop Robinson is the only player with notable NFL experience remaining. HC Hafley and GM Sullivan have both publicly stated the team is 'light' at edge rusher. This is the single most critical defensive need on the roster and remains at Tier 1 with maximum urgency for the draft (expect a Day 1 or early Day 2 pick here).

Tyreek Hill (8x Pro Bowler, WR1) released. Nick Westbrook-Ikhine released. The WR room is now just Jaylen Waddle (under contract through 2028) and young/unproven depth (Malik Washington, Tahj Washington). Losing Hill is catastrophic for the receiving corps. No WR additions have been made. With a new QB in Willis who needs weapons, WR has escalated from a pre-FA Tier 2 need to a Tier 1 need alongside EDGE. This team needs a legitimate WR2 who can start opposite Waddle.

Minkah Fitzpatrick was traded to the Jets for only a 2026 seventh-round pick — a heavily criticized return. He was Miami's best defensive back and graded as the 5th-best safety in the NFL per PFF. The Dolphins received almost nothing for him. The safety group is now completely unresolved — virtually every safety on the 2025 roster (Fitzpatrick, Ifeatu Melifonwu, Ashtyn Davis, Elijah Campbell) is a free agent with no replacements signed. Safety has escalated from Tier 2 to Tier 1.

James Daniels (starter, released after injury-plagued 2025), Cole Strange (signed with Chargers), Liam Eichenberg (released, failed physical) all gone. The interior OL had 3 significant departures with zero replacements. Aaron Brewer (C) and Austin Jackson (RT) remain under contract and provide the line's core, but guard positions are barren. GM Sullivan has stated the team is building 'inside-out' with trench focus as the philosophy. IOL escalates from Tier 2 to Tier 1.

No CB signings have been made. Virtually all experienced CBs (Rasul Douglas, Jack Jones, Kader Kohou, Artie Burns, A.J. Green) are UFAs with none re-signed. Only Ethan Bonner (ERFA tender, pure depth) was retained. Young players Storm Duck, Julius Brents, and Jason Marshall remain under contract but none has proven starting-caliber performance. CB remains a Tier 1 need with multiple starting spots to fill.

Greg Dulcich re-signed on a 1yr/$3.25M deal. He showed promise in 2025 (26 catches, 335 yards, 1 TD in 10 games). While the deal is short and not a long-term commitment, having a competent young TE under contract allows the team to address higher-priority needs (EDGE, WR, IOL, S, CB) first. TE drops slightly from Tier 2 to Tier 3 — it's still a need for the future but the Dulcich re-sign provides a functional band-aid for 2026.

Jason Sanders (franchise record-holder, 84.6% career FG%) was released. Miami has Riley Patterson on the roster as a UFA who may be brought back, but no kicker has been confirmed for 2026. Creates a new need that wasn't a priority before. Likely addressed in mid-to-late rounds of the draft or via a low-cost veteran signing.
Jake Bailey signed with the Falcons (3yr/$9M). Miami has no current punter on the roster. A replacement is needed but this is lowest-priority given the team's many high-tier needs.

key_additions.headline: Miami's new franchise QB; dual-threat starter follows HC Hafley from Green Bay on a genuine multi-year commitment
Productive young TE brought back on a prove-it deal after 26-catch, 335-yard debut with the Dolphins in 2025
Veteran rotational edge rusher retained as depth filler amid critical EDGE rebuild
Developmental CB retained via exclusive rights tender as Dolphins build youth at corner

key_losses.contract_summary: Released (post-June 1 designation); $67.4M dead cap in 2026, $31.8M in 2027
key_losses.headline: Franchise QB exit caps rebuild — record dead cap hit signals full regime reset under Sullivan/Hafley
8x Pro Bowler released while rehabbing torn ACL/dislocated knee; WR room now critically thin beyond Waddle

key_losses.contract_summary: Post-June 1 release; $23.8M dead cap, $20M cap savings
key_losses.headline: Team's leading pass rusher (8.5 sacks in 2025) gone, leaving EDGE as the most urgent need entering the draft

key_losses.contract_summary: Traded for 2026 7th-round pick; NYJ signed him 3yr/$40M
key_losses.headline: 3x All-Pro safety sent to division rival for a 7th-round pick in widely-criticized salary dump; safety room now empty
Starting guard departure compounds IOL crisis — Miami's interior line has zero proven starters under contract for 2026

key_losses.contract_summary: Traded to Eagles at 2025 deadline for 3rd-round pick; signed 4yr/$120M ($80M gtd) with Panthers in FA
key_losses.headline: Former first-round pick's departure in 2025 trade — now $120M Panther — hollowed out the pass rush alongside Chubb's release
Interior lineman follows Mike McDaniel to LA, further depleting Miami's already thin guard room
Miami's franchise-record punter departs after career-best 47.7-yard average in 2025

context.regime_change: Miami enters a full rebuild under new GM Jon-Eric Sullivan (from Green Bay Packers) and HC Jeff Hafley (also from Green Bay). The new regime has publicly committed to a 'draft-first, inside-out' building philosophy, avoiding high-cost veteran free agent signings. Sullivan has stated he wants to draft a QB 'every year if not every other year.'
context.draft_capital: Miami holds the No. 11 overall pick and significant draft capital (5 selections in the top 100) heading into the 2026 draft. The draft is expected to be the primary mechanism for addressing the roster's many critical needs.
context.dead_cap_context: The Tua Tagovailoa post-June 1 release creates a $67.4M dead cap hit in 2026, severely limiting Miami's ability to add meaningful veteran free agents. This is by design — the team is absorbing the pain in 2026 to have a clean cap in 2027 and beyond.
context.rebuild_assessment: Miami's 2026 free agency is almost entirely about subtraction (cap clearing) and one key addition (Willis). The Dolphins are not trying to compete in 2026. The rebuild is real, the holes are numerous, and the draft will need to address EDGE, WR, IOL, S, and CB — essentially the entire roster needs restocking.
